stem: You are the maternal-fetal medicine fellow seeing a 31-year-old G3P0 woman at 18 weeks gestation referred for a transvaginal cervical length scan. She has had two prior mid-trimester losses at 21 and 19 weeks with painless cervical dilatation noted on each occasion. There is no current contraction activity and no bleeding or fluid leak. The cervix appears closed externally.

data_table.Examination: Soft uterus, no contractions, speculum shows closed external os and no pooling
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal cervical length 18 mm with funnelling, no debris in amniotic fluid, fetus appropriately grown, anatomy normal
data_table.Medications: Pregnancy multivitamin only; no progesterone yet started

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intervention?

options.text: Arrange a transabdominal cerclage as the first-line intervention given recurrent loss and counsel for elective caesarean section at term
options.rationale: Transabdominal cerclage is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage or anatomical abnormalities; not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency Statement)

options.text: Recommend vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly without cerclage and arrange surveillance ultrasound every two weeks until twenty-four weeks gestation
options.rationale: Progesterone alone is insufficient with this clear history plus short cervix; cerclage has stronger evidence. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency Statement)

options.text: Offer an ultrasound-indicated McDonald cerclage with adjunctive vaginal progesterone and counsel about procedural and pregnancy risks
options.rationale: Singleton pregnancy with prior mid-trimester loss and short cervix (under 25 mm) before 24 weeks has clear evidence for ultrasound-indicated cerclage which halves preterm birth; vaginal progesterone is a reasonable adjunct. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency Statement)

options.text: Commence high-dose nifedipine prophylactically and arrange admission for inpatient bed rest until twenty-eight weeks gestation as the primary intervention
options.rationale: Prophylactic tocolytics and bed rest do not prevent preterm birth in cervical insufficiency and may cause harm. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency Statement)

options.text: Place a pessary in the outpatient clinic today and review in two weeks with transvaginal scan and counsel about preterm birth risk reduction
options.rationale: Cervical pessary evidence is mixed and inferior to cerclage in singletons with prior mid-trimester loss. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency Statement)

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency is suspected with prior mid-trimester painless loss. Singleton pregnancies with one or more prior preterm births and a short cervix on transvaginal ultrasound (under 25 mm before 24 weeks) benefit from ultrasound-indicated cerclage. History-indicated cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks is an alternative for three or more prior losses. Vaginal progesterone is often added. Bed rest is not recommended.
key_points: Ultrasound-indicated cerclage for short cervix + prior preterm birth halves recurrent preterm birth
Transabdominal cerclage reserved for failed transvaginal or anatomic abnormality
Vaginal progesterone is often combined; bed rest is not effective
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG distinguishes history-indicated (3 prior mid-trimester losses), ultrasound-indicated (1 to 2 prior preterm + cervix under 25 mm), and rescue cerclage (asymptomatic dilatation without infection); each has different evidence and timing.

stem: You are the community mental health team registrar reviewing a 28-year-old man four months after commencing olanzapine 15 mg nightly for a first episode of schizophrenia. He reports improved sleep and reduced positive symptoms. He has gained 8 kg, with new central adiposity and increased thirst. He smokes 20 cigarettes daily and has a family history of premature ischaemic heart disease.

data_table.Examination: Central adiposity, no acanthosis nigricans, no peripheral neuropathy
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 6.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.0%, triglycerides 2.6, HDL 0.9, LDL 3.4, ALT 64, prolactin 320 mIU/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch to an antipsychotic with a lower metabolic risk profile such as aripiprazole and continue lifestyle intervention with monitoring
options.rationale: Olanzapine has the highest metabolic risk and the patient meets at least three metabolic syndrome criteria; switching to a lower-risk agent (aripiprazole or ziprasidone) is RANZCP first-line. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Continue olanzapine and add metformin titrated to 1500 mg daily for weight management without any change to the antipsychotic regimen at this stage
options.rationale: Metformin may be considered as adjunct but switching the offending antipsychotic is the more impactful first step. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce olanzapine to 5 mg nightly to minimise side effects and reassess weight and metabolic parameters at the next six-monthly clinic review
options.rationale: Subtherapeutic doses risk relapse and do not reliably reverse metabolic effects. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Add clozapine at low dose as an augmentation strategy with weekly haematological monitoring and continue olanzapine at the current daily dose
options.rationale: Clozapine has highest metabolic risk of all antipsychotics and is for treatment-resistant schizophrenia, not as augmentation. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for bariatric surgery assessment and continue current antipsychotic regimen unchanged given the response to therapy for the primary psychiatric illness
options.rationale: Bariatric assessment is premature without first switching to a lower-risk antipsychotic and trialling lifestyle and metformin. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic-associated metabolic syndrome is a major contributor to premature mortality in schizophrenia (around 15 to 20 year reduction in life expectancy). Olanzapine and clozapine have the highest metabolic risk; aripiprazole, ziprasidone, and lurasidone have the lowest. RANZCP recommends baseline metabolic screening then every 3 months in first year, then 6-monthly. When metabolic syndrome develops, first-line is switching to a lower-risk agent before adding metformin.
key_points: Olanzapine and clozapine highest metabolic risk; aripiprazole and ziprasidone lowest
Switch antipsychotic first; add metformin if switch contraindicated or insufficient
Metabolic screening baseline, 3-monthly in year 1, then 6-monthly
explanation.pearls: Clozapine-induced metabolic effects can occur within the first 6 weeks; baseline weight, lipids, and glucose should be repeated at 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 48-year-old woman who smokes 25 cigarettes daily and wants to quit. Her past medical history includes major depressive disorder in remission on sertraline, well-controlled hypertension on perindopril, and stable angina with an ETT performed last year that was reassuring. She has tried nicotine patches alone twice without success.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable, no signs of depression on current review
data_table.Investigations: Recent FBC, UEC, LFTs all within normal range; ECG normal
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 100 mg, perindopril 5 mg, GTN PRN, aspirin 100 mg

lead_in: Which smoking cessation pharmacotherapy has the strongest evidence and is most appropriate here?

options.text: Recommend electronic cigarettes as primary smoking cessation aid given previous nicotine replacement therapy has failed in this patient with stable cardiac disease
options.rationale: Electronic cigarettes have limited evidence and are not first-line; RACGP recommends approved pharmacotherapy first. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Repeat nicotine patches alone at higher 21 mg dose and provide written information about quitting and a follow-up appointment in four weeks for review
options.rationale: Single-form NRT has failed twice; combination NRT or varenicline or bupropion should be offered. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Commence bupropion 150 mg daily titrated to twice daily after one week with weekly review to monitor for mood deterioration and seizure threshold change
options.rationale: Bupropion is a valid option but interacts with sertraline through serotonergic effects; varenicline is preferred when both are available. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Commence varenicline with structured dose titration over twelve weeks alongside behavioural support and counsel about possible neuropsychiatric monitoring
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective single agent and is no longer thought to increase neuropsychiatric risk per the EAGLES trial; appropriate with stable depression and behavioural support. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Refer to a residential drug and alcohol service for inpatient withdrawal management with intensive nursing and counselling provided over a four-week admission
options.rationale: Inpatient withdrawal for nicotine is not appropriate; community pharmacotherapy with behavioural support is first-line. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

explanation.summary: Varenicline is the most effective monotherapy for smoking cessation (OR around 2.9 vs placebo for 6-month abstinence). The EAGLES trial showed no significant neuropsychiatric harm in patients with stable psychiatric illness compared to NRT or placebo. Combination NRT (patch plus short-acting) is the next most effective. Bupropion is an alternative but carries seizure threshold lowering and serotonergic interaction risk with SSRIs. RACGP recommends pharmacotherapy + behavioural support for all motivated smokers.
key_points: Varenicline most effective monotherapy; standard 12-week course with structured titration
EAGLES trial showed neuropsychiatric safety in stable psychiatric illness
Combination NRT (patch + short-acting) is next most effective after varenicline
explanation.pearls: Varenicline dosing: 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily; start 1 to 2 weeks before quit date and continue for 12 weeks.

stem: You are the ED registrar managing a 24-year-old woman who presents 90 minutes after ingesting approximately 3 grams of her grandmother's amitriptyline in a deliberate self-harm context. She is drowsy with GCS 12. The intern shows you the ECG, which has triggered immediate concern for cardiotoxicity. She has had no seizures.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/52, HR 128, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 4 L/min, T 36.4
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, GCS E3 V4 M5, dry mucous membranes, dilated pupils, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: ECG: QRS 132 ms with terminal R wave in aVR over 3 mm, sinus tachycardia; venous pH 7.32, sodium 138, potassium 3.6, glucose 8.4

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube after airway protection without other immediate pharmacological intervention for the cardiac findings
options.rationale: Charcoal may be considered within 1 hour with airway protection but does not address established cardiotoxicity; alkalinisation is the time-critical action. (Source: TPCH Poisons Information)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate boluses titrated to QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.50 to 7.55 with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: QRS over 100 ms or terminal R in aVR over 3 mm indicates cardiotoxicity needing alkalinisation with sodium bicarbonate to overcome sodium channel blockade. (Source: TPCH Poisons Information)

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil to reverse central nervous system depression and assess for improvement in conscious state before further management
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed or unclear overdose due to seizure risk; it does not address TCA toxicity. (Source: TPCH Poisons Information)

options.text: Administer intravenous amiodarone 300 mg for the wide complex tachycardia and titrate further therapy to electrocardiograph response over the next hour
options.rationale: Class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmics including amiodarone can worsen TCA cardiotoxicity and are contraindicated. (Source: TPCH Poisons Information)

options.text: Administer intravenous physostigmine to reverse anticholinergic toxicity given the dilated pupils and tachycardia and follow with observation in the resuscitation bay
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose due to risk of asystole and seizures. (Source: TPCH Poisons Information)

explanation.summary: TCA toxicity is dominated by sodium channel blockade (wide QRS, terminal R in aVR), anticholinergic effects, alpha blockade (hypotension), and CNS depression with seizures. Sodium bicarbonate boluses targeting pH 7.50 to 7.55 overcome sodium channel blockade and are the cornerstone of treatment. Avoid amiodarone, flumazenil, and physostigmine. Lipid emulsion may be considered as rescue in refractory cardiotoxicity.
key_points: QRS over 100 ms or terminal R in aVR over 3 mm triggers sodium bicarbonate
Target pH 7.50 to 7.55 with boluses 1 to 2 mmol/kg
Avoid class IA, IC, III antiarrhythmics, flumazenil, and physostigmine
explanation.pearls: Intralipid 20% may be used as rescue therapy in refractory TCA cardiotoxicity (1.5 mL/kg bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion); contact Poisons Information for advice.

stem: You are the community mental health registrar reviewing a 56-year-old woman on lithium 1000 mg nightly for bipolar I disorder for 22 years. She has been stable on this dose. Her routine bloods show a steady deterioration in renal function over the past three years. She is otherwise well, has no polyuria or polydipsia, and lives independently. She does not want to risk relapse.

data_table.Examination: Fine tremor, no nystagmus, no ataxia
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level 0.7 mmol/L 12 hours post-dose, eGFR 42 mL/min/1.73 m^2 (52 one year ago, 64 three years ago), TSH 4.8, calcium 2.65, urine ACR 6
data_table.Medications: Lithium 1000 mg nightly, perindopril 5 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence high-dose amiloride for presumed nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and continue lithium at the current daily dose with routine monitoring
options.rationale: Amiloride is used for lithium-induced NDI but there is no polyuria here, and amiloride does not reverse chronic interstitial nephropathy. (Source: KHA-CARI Lithium Nephropathy Statement)

options.text: Stop lithium abruptly and commence sodium valproate at therapeutic dose with monitoring of mood and laboratory parameters over the next four weeks
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of lithium markedly increases relapse risk; valproate is contraindicated in women of reproductive potential without strict criteria. (Source: KHA-CARI Lithium Nephropathy Statement)

options.text: Continue current lithium dose and recheck renal function in three months without additional specialist input given the patient's wellness and longstanding stability
options.rationale: Progressive eGFR decline warrants escalation rather than watchful waiting; reaching CKD stage 4 risks irreversible disease. (Source: KHA-CARI Lithium Nephropathy Statement)

options.text: Increase fluid intake to four litres daily and recheck renal function in six months without making any changes to the current lithium dose or specialist input
options.rationale: Increased fluid alone does not reverse chronic interstitial nephropathy and risks further sodium dilution and lithium variability. (Source: KHA-CARI Lithium Nephropathy Statement)

options.text: Refer to nephrology and psychiatry jointly to plan a slow lithium taper or switch in the context of progressive lithium-associated kidney disease
options.rationale: Progressive eGFR decline with long-term lithium use suggests chronic interstitial nephritis; joint nephrology and psychiatry input plans a controlled taper or switch to balance relapse and renal risks. (Source: KHA-CARI Lithium Nephropathy Statement)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium causes chronic interstitial nephritis with progressive eGFR decline in around 20% of users after 20 years. Risk factors include duration over 10 years, episodes of toxicity, and lithium levels at the higher end. Joint specialist input is recommended once eGFR falls below 60 with progressive decline. Switching agents (valproate, lamotrigine, atypical antipsychotic) is balanced against relapse risk, which is highest after lithium discontinuation.
key_points: Long-term lithium causes chronic interstitial nephritis in around 20% of patients after 20 years
Joint nephrology and psychiatry input when eGFR falling below 60 with progressive decline
Slow taper preferred; abrupt cessation increases relapse risk substantially
explanation.pearls: Lithium also causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, hyperparathyroidism with hypercalcaemia, and hypothyroidism; check calcium, PTH, TSH, urinary concentrating ability annually.
